senses.definition: to prevent trouble by trying not to become involved in something or draw attention to oneself

examples.x: In a tough neighborhood, it's advisable to keep your head down to avoid unnecessary confrontations.
During times of political unrest, journalists often have to keep their heads down to ensure their safety while reporting on the ground.
After the embarrassing incident at work, Sarah decided to keep her head down and focus on her tasks to regain her colleagues' trust.
The undercover detective had to keep his head down while infiltrating the criminal organization to gather information discreetly.
In a competitive job market, it's essential to keep your head down, work hard, and prove your skills to advance in your career.

senses.definition: to try to stay away from trouble

examples.x: To secure a promotion at the company, Mark resolved to keep his nose clean, adhering strictly to company policies and ethical standards.
After serving time in prison, John was determined to keep his nose clean and lead a lawful life upon his release.
The athlete knew that maintaining peak performance required more than talent; he also needed to keep his nose clean by avoiding negative influences.
As a public figure, Sarah had to be cautious and keep her nose clean to maintain her pristine image and reputation.
The young student was always diligent in class, making an effort to keep his nose clean by avoiding any form of cheating or misconduct.

senses.definition: to decide to be no longer involved with something in any way

examples.x: Dunbar has already washed his hands of the project.
I’ve washed my hands of the whole affair.
She wanted to wash her hands of the contentious argument and avoid further involvement.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv hˈoʊld wˈɑːnt nˈoʊ lˈɪɾəl tɹˈʌk wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to refuse to get involved with someone or something in any way

examples.x: Sarah had no truck with the gossip at the office, preferring to focus on her work and avoid office drama.
The environmental activist held no truck with companies that didn't prioritize sustainability and conservation efforts.
John wanted little truck with his neighbor's constant interference in his personal life and decisions.
The strict teacher had no truck with students who didn't follow classroom rules and policies.
The political reformer wanted no truck with corrupt politicians and was determined to bring about change.

senses.definition: to only concern oneself with one's own private matters and not to interfere in those of others

examples.x: I asked Laura what was wrong and she told me to mind my own business.

senses.definition: to refrain from interfering in someone else's private affairs

examples.x: Why don't you mind your own beeswax and stop asking about my personal life?"
When Sarah's younger brother kept poking around in her room, she told him, "You should mind your own beeswax, little buddy!"
The manager overheard office gossip and reminded the employees to mind their own beeswax and stay focused on their tasks.
During the family dinner, Tom's uncle made unsolicited comments about his career, prompting Tom to assert, "I can mind my own beeswax when it comes to my job."
At the neighborhood block party, John tactfully told his neighbors to mind their own beeswax when they offered unsolicited advice about his home renovation project.

senses.definition: used to tell someone that it is not necessary for them to know about something and that they should stop questioning about it

examples.x: When asked about her savings, Mary politely replied, "That's none of your business."
Jim's mother reminded him that his sister's decision to change careers was entirely her choice and none of his business.
When the nosy neighbor inquired about their relationship status, Sarah told her partner, "Let's just say it's none of her business."
During a heated argument, Tom bluntly told his friend, "What I do with my free time is none of your business."
At the office, Jane avoided discussing her personal life with her colleagues, firmly asserting that it was none of their business.

word_list.pronunciation: nˌɑːt tˈʌtʃ ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ wɪð ɐ tˈɛnfˈʊt pˈoʊl

senses.definition: to have nothing to do with someone or something

examples.x: After the recent controversies, the company's reputation is so tarnished that potential investors wouldn't touch it with a ten-foot pole.
The politician was so unpopular that many voters wouldn't touch his campaign with a ten-foot pole.
Knowing how unreliable the used car dealership was, Sarah wouldn't touch their offers with a ten-foot pole.
The restaurant's health code violations were so severe that even the most daring food enthusiasts wouldn't touch the place with a ten-foot pole.

senses.definition: in a state where one notices something but refuses to get oneself involved in it

examples.x: When it comes to the company's strategic decisions, John feels more comfortable offering advice from the sidelines rather than taking the lead.
As a parent, Lisa often finds herself on the sidelines, supporting her children in their pursuits while letting them make their own choices.
When the project faced challenges, Susan chose to step back and watch from the sidelines, allowing her team to brainstorm solutions.

word_list.pronunciation: wˈɜːm wˈiːzəl wˈʌnz wˈeɪ ˌaʊɾəv ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to avoid doing something by making excuses or being dishonest

examples.x: When confronted with his overdue assignment, the student tried to worm his way out of it by claiming his dog had eaten his homework.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡˈɪv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ ɐ wˈaɪd bˈɜːθ

senses.definition: to not become involved with or go near someone or something

examples.x: After the heated argument, John decided to give his colleague a wide berth to avoid further confrontation.
When hiking in the wilderness, it's wise to give wild animals a wide berth to ensure your safety.
Sarah chose to give the controversial topic a wide berth during the family gathering to maintain a peaceful atmosphere.
In the bustling marketplace, tourists were advised to give pickpockets a wide berth to protect their belongings.
To prevent illness during flu season, it's essential to practice good hygiene and give sick individuals a wide berth.

word_list.pronunciation: ɐvˈɔɪd ɔːɹ ɪvˈeɪd ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ lˈaɪk ðə plˈeɪɡ

senses.definition: to do one's best to avoid someone or something completely

examples.x: Knowing how difficult she can be to work with, Jim avoids his coworker like the plague.
During the flu season, it's essential to avoid crowded places like the plague to minimize the risk of getting sick.
After the disastrous investment, the entrepreneur decided to evade high-risk ventures like the plague.

word_list.pronunciation: sˈiː ðə bˈæk ɔːɹ lˈæst ʌv ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to finally manage to get rid of someone or something

examples.x: Tom had struggled with his old, unreliable car for years, so he was pleased to see the back of it when he finally bought a new one.
Sarah couldn't wait to see the back of the never-ending construction noise outside her apartment.
When he left the company, we were relieved to see the last of him.
She moved to a different city, so we've probably seen the last of our noisy neighbor.

senses.definition: to have no right to be in a certain place or do a certain thing

examples.x: When the meeting topic was confidential financial matters, Sarah had no business being in the room.
John had no business interrupting the professor's lecture with his irrelevant questions.
It's not your project, so you have no business making decisions about it.
During the dispute between two neighbors, the third neighbor had no business taking sides and making it worse.
As an outsider, Mark had no business meddling in the family's personal matters and disagreements.

senses.definition: to have no desire to get involved in something
(of a person or thing) no longer involved or present in a situation or activity

examples.x: After the company's restructuring, Sarah found herself out of the picture, with no role in the new organization.
When the team decided on a new direction for the project, John's proposal was completely out of the picture.
The controversial idea was quickly dismissed and pushed out of the picture during the brainstorming session.
As the family business transitioned to a new generation, Mark realized he was gradually moving out of the picture.
The sudden policy change left many employees feeling out of the picture, as their input was no longer valued.
